text: Chapter 2 “Incompatible Ideas” In this chapter we seek to explain the disconnect between the ideals that underlie the rhetoric of equality and democracy that underpins Canadian public schooling and the prohibition on peace from the “wrong” side of the gate that young people encounter when they are involved in anti-violence and conflict management programs in schools in Toronto’s inner city. Using data from the case studies, we explore why schooling at the very sites where young people experience considerable adversity to peace ends up being focused on maintaining a variant of negative peace that ensures that any transgressions are detected, punishment meted out and offenders rendered peaceful, if not safe, through mechanisms of intense surveillance, disciplinary techniques and peacekeeping that privileges the cultural worldview of those who are privileged. Moreover, these schooling practices reflect the times in which these activities are occurring (e.g. during a period of substantial cuts to publicly funded social programs) and the growing imposition of a common curriculum upon divergent instructional approaches. School peace initiatives that limit their activities to enforcement of prohibitions on deviance rather than creating the conditions in which young people and their adult caretakers are empowered to explore their understandings of the sources of violence in their communities in a shared way that is designed to raise critical awareness and generate solutions to the sources of that violence risk legitimizing those very structures of inequality that schooling on its most abstract level promises to remove. In other words the enforcement of schooling without any opportunities for participatory dialogue in which young people, people can work out whether peace as envisioned by those with power is what is wanted by those on the margins of societal life maintains rather than challenges existing patterns of racism and sexism.
explanation: The passage shows domain-specific academic reasoning with nuanced critique of education policy, and the prose appears cohesive and well-structured, which is more characteristic of human scholarly writing; however, very advanced AI could reproduce this style.
